metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC with a concrete proving command that asserts `_config_sources.py` contains no imports from `rebar` packages other than `rebar._config_schema` and `rebar._deprecations` (e.g., `grep -E '^(import|from) rebar' src/rebar/_config_sources.py | grep -Ev '(rebar\._config_schema|rebar\._deprecations)' | diff - /dev/null`).
evidence: Plan states: 'The leaf imports ONLY stdlib + rebar._config_schema + rebar._deprecations (no import of rebar.config), so it is a true leaf with no back-edge.'
No AC verifies this property with a grep or AST-based command.
The config test suite catches circular imports but not non-circular constraint violations.
findings.finding: The leaf-import constraint — that `_config_sources.py` imports ONLY stdlib + `rebar._config_schema` + `rebar._deprecations` — is a load-bearing architectural claim (it makes the module a true leaf with no back-edge) but has no proving command in the AC set. A circular import would be caught by the test suite, but a non-circular constraint violation (e.g., importing `rebar.utils` or `rebar.logging`) would silently pass all current ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers the leaf-import constraint)
scenarios: An implementer accidentally imports a rebar utility from _config_sources.py; all ACs pass (no circular import), but the architectural constraint is violated.
A future change adds an import to _config_sources.py without noticing the constraint, since CI has no guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'No disallowed rebar imports in `_config_sources.py`; verify: `! grep -E "^(import|from) rebar" src/rebar/_config_sources.py | grep -Ev "(rebar\._config_schema|rebar\._deprecations)"`'

findings.finding: The plan body explicitly promises that _config_sources.py 'imports ONLY stdlib + rebar._config_schema + rebar._deprecations (no import of rebar.config)' — a structural invariant that prevents a circular-import regression — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this constraint. Behavioral tests (AC #7) cannot catch a stray import that doesn't break test logic.
findings.location: 'New seam' section (leaf import constraint) + Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC)
findings.suggested_fix:
Insert after AC #1:
- [ ] `_config_sources.py` does not import `rebar.config`; verify: `! grep -E 'from rebar\.config|import rebar\.config' src/rebar/_config_sources.py`
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an entry (or update an existing section) in `docs/architecture.md` documenting the three-layer config module structure (`_config_schema` → `_config_sources` → `config.py`), each layer's responsibility, and the leaf-import constraint for `_config_sources.py`.
evidence: Plan says: 'DELETES that stale row rather than claiming it records the seam below'
Plan introduces new leaf `src/rebar/_config_sources.py` with explicit import constraints and a defined responsibility ('turns files+env into raw sparse mappings')
No AC or step adds a new arch.md row/section describing the three-module structure
T5e documentation sub-check: 'a novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design doc update'
findings.finding: The plan deletes the stale arch.md remedy row for config.py but does not add a replacement entry documenting the resulting three-layer config architecture (_config_schema.py → _config_sources.py → config.py). Future maintainers have no authoritative record of the new module boundaries, their responsibilities, or the leaf-import constraint.
findings.location: docs/architecture.md / AC #5 ('The stale config.py remedy row is gone')
scenarios: A future contributor adding config functionality doesn't know which layer to put it in
A reviewer cannot tell whether a new import in _config_sources.py is a violation of the leaf constraint without finding the plan ticket
The arch.md offenders table becomes inconsistent — config.py row removed but no companion row for the new structure
findings.suggested_fix: In the architecture.md refactor section (or a new 'Config subsystem' subsection), add: '_config_sources.py (leaf): raw-input resolution — filesystem discovery, TOML parsing/cache, env-override collection; imports only stdlib + _config_schema + _deprecations. _config_schema.py (leaf): typed dataclass/schema layer. config.py (facade): typed-merge, precedence, resolved-Config cache, public API; re-exports _config_sources symbols for back-compat.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Simplify the allowlist AC verify command to remove the dead `grep -qv` clause, leaving only: `! grep -q "src/rebar/config.py" .github/module-size-allowlist.txt`.
evidence: VCDT type: fixture invalidity / dead clause — `grep -qv` exits 0 for any file with >1 entry regardless of whether the target string is present.
The second clause (`! grep -q ...`) is the sole operative check.
findings.finding: The allowlist-removal verify command has a dead first clause: `grep -qv "src/rebar/config.py" .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` exits 0 whenever any line in the file does NOT match the pattern — which is always true for a multi-entry allowlist. This makes the first clause of the `&&` expression permanently true, reducing the compound check to just its second clause. The check is net-correct (the second clause does the real work) but the dead clause obscures intent and could mislead reviewers into thinking two independent conditions are being tested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 3: config.py removed from allowlist
scenarios: The allowlist still contains the config.py entry; the first clause passes (other entries exist), and only the second clause correctly fails — so the bug is harmless today but could mislead a reader into thinking the command checks two things.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the verify command with: `! grep -q 'src/rebar/config.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Simplify AC3's verify command to the single meaningful check: `! grep -q "src/rebar/config.py" .github/module-size-allowlist.txt`
evidence: The first clause (`grep -qv pattern file`) returns exit 0 if any line does NOT match the pattern — true as long as the file has any other entry — making it a no-op that never fails. Only the second clause performs the actual removal check.
findings.finding: AC3's verify command contains a logically meaningless first clause: `grep -qv "src/rebar/config.py" .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` exits 0 whenever ANY other line exists in the file (regardless of whether config.py is still present), so it provides no signal about removal and could mislead an implementer into trusting a passing check when config.py is still listed.
scenarios: An implementer runs the verify command after a partial/failed refactor; config.py is still listed; the first clause passes (file has other lines), and only the second clause catches the failure — but the compound expression still fails, so the net behavior is correct. However, if an implementer uses only the first clause for manual spot-checking (copy-paste error), they will get a false pass.
A code-reviewer reading the plan may assume both clauses are meaningful and not notice that the first adds nothing, reducing plan clarity.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC3 verify line with:
`verify: ! grep -q "src/rebar/config.py" .github/module-size-allowlist.txt`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/env-vars.md` to the edit set (or explicitly acknowledge it as intentionally deferred), since its `src/rebar/config.py` source-file column entries and line-number references for moved symbols will be stale after the split.
evidence: docs/env-vars.md:165-168 lists src/rebar/config.py line numbers for dynamic os.environ.get calls that will move to _config_sources.py
Plan section 'Scope note' and 'Back-compat' make no mention of docs/env-vars.md
findings.finding: docs/env-vars.md contains source-file column entries pointing to 'src/rebar/config.py' (with line numbers) for several symbols being moved to _config_sources.py — including REBAR_TRACKER_DIR, XDG_CONFIG_HOME, REBAR_CONFIG, REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS, and the alias-resolver env vars. After the split, these originate from _config_sources.py, making the source-file references stale. This file is not mentioned in the plan's edit set or acknowledged as a known-stale consumer.
scenarios: An executor following the plan exactly updates config.py and creates _config_sources.py but does not touch docs/env-vars.md, leaving line-number references pointing to wrong file and wrong lines.
The docs/env-vars.md line-number references (e.g. config.py:492, config.py:505, config.py:509, config.py:516) will point to functions that no longer exist at those locations in config.py post-split.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a plan step to regenerate/update docs/env-vars.md (if it is generated from a script) or add an AC that verifies the env-vars doc is updated; alternatively, explicitly note it as a known-deferred follow-up.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
